retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Blood Brain Barrier -- Structure and Function -- Pericytes (PCs)
retrieved_docs.content: Embedded within the capillary basement membrane, PCs play a crucial role in angiogenesis, the structural integrity and differentiation of the microvessels, and the formation of endothelial tight junctions. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Blood Brain Barrier -- Cellular Level -- Mural Cells
retrieved_docs.content: Mural cells include vascular smooth muscle cells and pericytes (PCs), which incompletely cover the endothelial walls of the microvasculature. PCs extend long cellular processes along the abluminal surface of the endothelium that contain contractile proteins, allowing them to contract and control the capillary diameter. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Arterioles -- Structure and Function -- The Adventitia
retrieved_docs.content: The adventitial layer consists of fibroblasts, an extracellular matrix, and thick bundles of collagen fibers organized along the longitudinal axis of the blood vessel. [3] Evidence suggests that some adventitial fibroblasts may be considered stem mesenchymal progenitor cells. [4] Nonmyelinated free nerve endings are distributed in the adventitial layer approximately 5 µm from the outermost part of vascular smooth muscle. [5] Recent studies have shown that elastic fibers arranged longitudinally in the outer layer allow arterioles to elongate or recoil in expandable tissues such as skeletal muscle. Arterioles within these expandable tissues usually possess additional external elastic lamina, absent in non-expandable tissues like the brain. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Blood Brain Barrier -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: All blood vessels are made up of two distinct cell types, ECs and mural cells, with the latter composed of vascular smooth muscle cells and pericytes. While ECs are the main component of the BBB, allowing for high selectivity, the interactions between ECs and mural cells allow for induction and maintenance of the BBB. Other vital interactions include ECs and immune cells, neural cells, and glial cells. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Blood Vascular System  -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Pericytes are fundamental cells for the health, formation, and function of blood vessels. The latter was discovered in the 19th century by Rouget, but Zimmerman renamed them pericytes by Zimmermann. Depending on their location, they take on different roles and importance; in the brain and retina, they become fundamental to build the blood-retinal barrier and the blood-brain barrier. In the kidneys, they influence the functionality of the glomeruli for filtration; in the liver vessels, they are essential for the remodeling of the extracellular matrix. Recent evidence demonstrates their role in vessel immune response and phagocytic activity. The blood vessel must always be considered different, depending on the anatomical area and the demand of the tissue for the same blood vessel.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Blood Vascular System  -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Capillaries have the smallest vessel diameter, which ranges from 4 to 10 microns. Capillaries have the most reduced vessel wall, comprised of endothelium surrounded by connective tissue. There is a basement membrane that houses pericytes. Pericytes are essential neurovascular signaling cells and respond to blood pressure changes. Capillaries are also highly specialized vessels, with different tunica features dependent on anatomic relationships and function. There are three types of capillaries with highly specialized functions. They are anatomically isolated to respective regions of the body. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Blood Vascular System  -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Housed in the basement membrane of the smallest vessels in the body, both capillaries and venules are pericytes. Pericytes, also known as Rouget cells, are circumferentially arranged inside the microvascular wall and communicate via both direct contact to the endothelial cells and paracrine signaling. They are essential in regulating the maturation of the endothelial cells and are crucial components of the neurovascular unit. Pericytes aid in maintaining microvascular blood flow, clearing cellular debris, and maintaining the integrity of the blood-brain barrier.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bone Markings -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The bone capillary network fills the marrow cavity, predominantly comprising dense, fenestrated, and branched sinusoidal vessels. Linear columnar vessels predominate in the metaphysis and endosteum, interconnected by loops or arches. These vessels drain into a central vein in long bone diaphyses, with smaller veins branching off to periosteal veins. Type H vessels, expressing markers like endomucin and CD31, exhibit higher oxygen levels and blood velocity due to direct arteriolar connections, influencing tissue microenvironments. Various cell types surrounding blood vessels, like pericytes and stromal cells, show distinct characteristics depending on the vessel type and share similarities with mesenchymal progenitor cells, suggesting a role in tissue repair. These cells contribute to bone lineage during development and adulthood, supporting diverse cellular environments within bone tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Middle Cerebral Artery -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The development of the MCA reflects the integration of genetic programming and the metabolic demands of the embryonic brain. The MCA arises during early cerebrovascular morphogenesis, a process involving vasculogenesis and angiogenesis. Vasculogenesis establishes the primitive vascular plexus as angioblasts form endothelial tubes within the meninx primitiva, a connective tissue layer enveloping the neural tube. Angiogenesis, driven by hypoxia-induced factors such as vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and stromal cell-derived factor 1 (SDF1), promotes vessel sprouting to meet the brain's increasing metabolic demands. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Great Vessel -- Cellular
retrieved_docs.content: Two types of vascular cells derive from mural cells. The first is the pericyte, which affects more small vessels, while the second type, vascular smooth muscle cells, are found, especially in large vessels. Pericytes play immune functions and can behave like stem cells. Vascular smooth muscle cells are involved in the function of vasoconstriction and vasodilation of the vessels (blood pressure and distribution of blood to the tissues). These cells also have the task of maintaining the morphology of the vessel.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Arterioles -- Clinical Significance -- Cerebral Circulation
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral blood flow is mostly regulated by the arterial partial pressure of CO 2 (pC0 2 ). If arterial pO 2 is normal or above normal, cerebral blood flow is proportional to arterial pCO 2 . High pCO 2 induces cerebral arteriolar vasodilation, thus increasing cerebral blood flow. This unique characteristic of cerebral arterioles allows therapeutic hyperventilation for lowering intracranial pressure (ICP) when indicated. Furthermore, the final step of blood flow regulation by the arterioles matches the focal demand of nearby tissue, which is mediated by microvascular pericytes. [39] [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Periosteum And Endosteum -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The inner cellular layer of the periosteum is also described as the inner cambium layer by some authors. It is made up of osteoprogenitor cells (fibroblast-like cells); it is also known as the osteogenetic layer. [14] The Inner layer contains osteoblasts in young developing bones. [14] [17] Although in adult bones, osteoblasts may be absent, they appear whenever required (e.g., fracture healing). [12] Osteoprogenitor cells are multipotent stem cells; they can undergo mitotic division and differentiate into osteoblasts by taking up thymidine. [17] [18] [17] The inner layer is also a rich vascular structure with many microvessels; stimulated pericytes derived from the endothelium of these microvessels may augment the osteoblast formation from the osteoprogenitor cells. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Periventricular and Intraventricular Hemorrhage -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Spontaneous rupture of the germinal matrix vessels may occur from hypoxia as a consequence of fluctuation in cerebral blood flow. [6] [15] The germinal matrix is more prone to hemorrhage in premature infants during the first 48 to 72 hours of life. The structural fragility of the germinal matrix is what leads to PIVH. Due to immaturity, endothelial tight junctions, basement membrane, pericytes, fibronectin, and astrocyte end-feet may be defective. [6] The parenchyma of basal lamina is relatively soft and fragile because of deficient fibronectin and collagen. Intracranial vasculature of preterm neonates has the same innate immaturity as the vessels in other organs, which means that the walls are much weaker than in adults and are more prone to rupture. The cross-sectional area of the blood vessels is the largest in the human germinal matrix. Decreased expression of glial fibrillary acidic protein in the germinal matrix is very likely to reduce the strength of the cytoskeletal structure. The structural variants of subependymal veins are also confirmed to bring about the brittleness of the germinal matrix, as well as the inclination of thrombosis. The high vascularization adds to the fragility of the germinal matrix as well, especially when the neonate encounters hypoxia. Furthermore, the premature vasculature lacks the autoregulation to modulate the lumen under fluctuant hemodynamics. [6] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Blood Brain Barrier -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The BBB is composed of cerebral capillary wall endothelial cells (ECs) held together via tight junctions (TJs). These TJs, surrounded by pericytes, astrocytes, and the basal lamina, contribute to the highly selective nature of the BBB, limiting the passage of substances from the blood to the brain more so than any other capillaries in the body. [2] See Figure. Blood Brain Barrier.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Mammillary Bodies -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: Lymphatics within the brain differ from other regions of the body. Within the brain, the blood-brain barrier regulates the movement/transport of nutrients, small molecules, metabolites, and/or cells and toxins in and out of the brain.  The blood-brain barrier is a functional and structural boundary between vasculature and the brain that forms from brain vasculature endothelial cells, adjacent astrocytic end-feet, and pericytes. Tight junctions connect brain endothelial cells and create a physical barrier. This barrier forces most molecules to travel through selective transcellular routes – forming a transport barrier. In addition, there is also a synergistic relationship between brain endothelial cells and astrocytes. Perivascular astrocytic end-feet surround brain endothelial cells, but a basal lamina separates them. The barrier formed from these astrocytic end-feet and endothelial cells protect the brain from toxins and assist with transport of nutrients, wastes, glucose, lipids, and neurotransmitters between capillaries and neurons in the brain. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Arterioles -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Oxygenated arterial blood circulates through the body via the vascular tree consisting of sequentially smaller arteries, arterioles, and capillary beds. Nutrients and waste exchange between the blood and body tissues occurs at the capillary bed. Venules serve as exit vessels in the capillary bed of various organs and unite to form veins, which return the blood to the heart. The arteriolar wall consists of 3 layers of cellular and extracellular components. The intima contains endothelial cells on a basement membrane; the tunica media consists of internal elastic lamina and layers of smooth muscle; and the outer adventitia is made mainly of collagen, nerve endings, and fibroblasts. Arterioles contribute to maintaining mean arterial pressure and tissue perfusion as they are the important sites of total peripheral resistance. By increasing or decreasing the diameter, they also play a principal role in regulating blood flow in an organ or tissue-specific manner.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: and peripheral/visceral atherosclerosis, all of which contribute to the risk of ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion, illustrating the systemic impact of vascular disease on cerebral blood flow. These entities are organized hierarchically: general cardiac and vascular diagnoses and risk factors feed into specific diagnostic categories; diagnostic categories give rise to biopsy techniques; biopsy techniques serve as parents for detailed ventricular or pericardial biopsy procedures; biopsies and excisions connect to broader operative groups such as “HEART: GENERAL AND MISCELLANEOUS OPERATIVE PROCEDURES” and “other or heart procedures.” This integrated framework delineates the relationships among anatomical structures (heart, pericardium, papillary muscles), diagnostic modalities, therapeutic interventions, and related disease conditions, supporting comprehensive cardiac and vascular patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2454
retrieved_docs.content: The Vascular System is the anatomical region implicated in various cerebrovascular and arterial disorders. It is identified as the anatomic site associated with other and ill-defined cerebrovascular disease, reflecting a range of poorly characterized cerebrovascular pathologies that involve the brain’s blood vessels. Additionally, the Vascular System is the site of تسلخ الشرايين, an arterial ulceration condition that denotes damage to the arterial wall. Recognizing the Vascular System as the common anatomical substrate for these conditions underscores its central role in the pathophysiology of cerebrovascular disease and arterial injury, informing diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: detachment of the eye (انفصال مشيمية العين) and hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي). Peripheral involvement includes peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis and peripheral focal retinitis with retinochoroiditis. Across all entities, the choroidea (choroid) is the anatomical locus of the characteristic lesions, underscoring its central role in pathophysiology, clinical assessment, precise diagnosis, and tailored management of choroidal and retinal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2448
retrieved_docs.content: The structure of intracranial artery serves as the anatomical site where the pathological finding termed تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial arterial sclerosis) is identified, indicating that this form of arterial sclerosis occurs within the brain’s arterial architecture and is relevant for diagnostic and therapeutic considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1082
retrieved_docs.content: Moreover, diagnostic observations and pathological findings related to the choroidea are specifically localized to the site termed “حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي,” indicating a genetically related placental structure. Precise identification of choroidal involvement at this site is essential for accurate diagnosis, management, and prognostication of the diverse ocular diseases that arise within the choroidea.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2228
retrieved_docs.content: The vascular system is identified as the anatomic site associated with other and ill‑defined cerebrovascular disease, indicating that these cerebrovascular conditions involve abnormalities or pathology of the blood vessels that supply the brain.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2087
retrieved_docs.content: Other and ill-defined cerebrovascular disease and late effects of cerebrovascular disease are both classified under the broader category اضطراب مخي وعائي (cerebrovascular disorder). Within this hierarchy, other and ill-defined cerebrovascular disease is specifically linked to تَصَلُّبٌ شِرْيَانِيّ (fibrous sclerosis), indicating that some poorly defined cerebrovascular conditions may involve fibrotic changes. Recognizing that these subtypes fall under اضطراب مخي وعائي and that other and ill-defined cerebrovascular disease can be associated with fibrous sclerosis helps clinicians organize cerebrovascular diagnoses and anticipate potential fibrotic complications in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 266
retrieved_docs.content: and retina, leading to scarring, atrophy, detachment, neovascularization and visual impairment. Accurate identification of the specific choroidal and retinal pathology is essential for comprehensive evaluation, multidisciplinary management and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2817
retrieved_docs.content: The Vascular System serves as the anatomical site associated with both other and ill‑defined cerebrovascular disease and اضطراب الأوعية الكلوية (renal vascular disorder), indicating that abnormalities in the vascular network can underlie diverse cerebrovascular and renal vessel pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 236
retrieved_docs.content: (ندبة مشيمية شبكية). Hemorrhagic choroidal manifestations are noted as choroidal hemorrhage and massive choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي باثق). Perivascular deposits (بُقَعُ روث) are also recorded. Collectively, these entities illustrate the intersection of systemic vascular disease with specific retinal and choroidal hemorrhagic findings, neovascular disorders, inflammatory and infectious processes, traumatic and postoperative sequelae, and degenerative changes, providing a comprehensive framework for diagnosis, therapeutic decision‑making, and prognostic assessment in ophthalmic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1238
retrieved_docs.content: malformation of the choroid (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), partial perimacular choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي), and hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي). Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroid is a central site for diverse pathological processes, and recognizing the specific type of choroidal involvement—whether inflammatory, scar‑forming, degenerative, traumatic, metastatic, or congenital—is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 977
retrieved_docs.content: APMPPE) and familial choroidal neovascularization (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية). Additional specific sub‑conditions listed under the same category are انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي, تنكس حصوي, and حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي. Together, these entities illustrate the extensive and heterogeneous nature of choroidal and retinal disorders, underscoring the importance of precise classification for effective patient care, targeted research, and accurate medical coding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1184
retrieved_docs.content: Other and ill-defined cerebrovascular disease is mapped to تصلب الشرايين المخية (cerebral arterial sclerosis), and تصلب الشرايين المخية has a relationship with خَرَف احتشائي متعدّد (multiple lacunar infarcts). This indicates that cerebral arterial sclerosis is classified within the broader category of other and ill‑defined cerebrovascular disease and is associated with the development of multiple lacunar strokes, underscoring its relevance in the assessment and management of small‑vessel cerebrovascular pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 242
retrieved_docs.content: other and ill‑defined cerebrovascular disease, اضطراب الأوعية الكلوية (renal vessel disorder), and تسلخ الشرايين (arterial ulceration). Together, these relationships illustrate how dialysis‑related arteriovenous shunt creation and revision, a wide spectrum of catheter‑based cardiovascular interventions, hemodynamic monitoring, and specific cerebrovascular conditions are organized within the broader vascular and cardiovascular systems, with the term cévy linking procedural sites across multiple contexts and aligning with regulatory taxonomy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: The retina serves as the primary anatomic site for a variety of ocular pathologies, including retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusions, and retinopathy. In patients with secondary diabetes mellitus, both non‑proliferative retinopathy and moderate non‑proliferative retinopathy are identified as retinal findings, underscoring that diabetic retinopathy manifests within the retinal tissue. Additionally, the condition referred to as داء ريفسام also presents its findings in the retina. Recognizing the retina as the common location for these disorders is essential for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and management in ophthalmic and systemic disease care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2634
retrieved_docs.content: (تنكس حصوي) and choroidal atrophy (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), while localized calcification around the optic disc (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) represents a specific form of mineral deposition. Finally, choroidal scarring (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) denotes fibrotic remodeling after injury or inflammation. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroidea’s central role in ocular disease, highlighting the need for careful clinical assessment and targeted management of choroidal involvement across diverse pathological contexts.
